root_query: antimicrobial peptide antibacterial MIC minimum inhibitory concentration broth microdilution experimental

databases.name: DBAASP v3 — Database of Antimicrobial Activity and Structure of Peptides
databases.access_method: REST API (https://dbaasp.org/api/v1) and Advanced Search CSV/JSON/FASTA export; no authentication required

extraction_strategy.notes: Use /api/v1/peptides with target_group_id for bacteria; filter by activity_measure_id for MIC. Supports output as JSON, FASTA, CSV. Alternatively, use Advanced Search UI and export CSV. The DBAASP_api_helper_libraries GitHub repo (melomcr/dbaasp_api_helper_libraries) provides wrapper functions. Each peptide record contains a TargetActivity list with per-pathogen MIC values — one record per activity row.

databases.notes: Primary database source. DBAASP stores MIC per peptide–pathogen pair with assay conditions (medium, CFU, temperature), matching our schema fields exactly. Contains >15,700 entries as of v3 (2021); continuously updated. Manually curated from primary literature with PubMed links. Covers ribosomal, non-ribosomal, and synthetic peptides. API query for antibacterial MICs: https://dbaasp.org/api/v1?query=search&target_group_id=1&format=json

databases.name: DRAMP 4.0 — Data Repository of Antimicrobial Peptides
databases.access_method: bulk download from http://dramp.cpu-bioinfor.org/downloads/ (categorized by activity type); also on figshare and GitHub (CPU-DRAMP/DRAMP-3.0)

extraction_strategy.notes: Download the 'antibacterial' subset from the downloads page (categorized by activity). The general dataset contains experimentally validated entries with MIC values and target organism annotations. Patent entries may lack assay-condition detail — prefer general dataset. PubMed IDs in activity fields allow tracing to primary papers.

databases.notes: Second major database source. DRAMP 4.0 (2024) contains 30,260 entries: 11,612 general, 17,886 patent, 96 clinical. The general dataset (~11k) is the target. DRAMP has ~70% non-overlapping sequences vs APD+CAMP, providing coverage complementary to DBAASP. CC BY 4.0 license permits unrestricted use with citation. DRAMP 4.0 adds serum/protease stability annotations not in DBAASP.

databases.name: CAMPR4 — Collection of Anti-Microbial Peptides (version 4)
databases.access_method: web search with export; search by target organism or MIC activity; no bulk API; export individual query results

extraction_strategy.notes: Query by target organism (e.g., Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus) and export results. MIC values are stored per target organism. CAMPR4 has 24,243 sequences (11,827 natural + 12,416 synthetic). Focus on experimentally validated (non-predicted, non-patent) entries with MIC values.

databases.notes: Tertiary database source. CAMPR4 uses a separate curation pipeline from DBAASP and DRAMP, capturing records the others may miss. Contains information on N/C terminal modifications and unusual amino acids. Lower priority than DBAASP and DRAMP due to no bulk download API, but provides a useful cross-check.

scientific_papers.name: Luo et al. 2023 — Comparative Evaluation of AMPs for Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

extraction_strategy.notes: Table 1 (p.4): peptide names and sequences for all 11 AMPs. Table 2 (p.5): MIC values in µg/mL for E. coli, P. aeruginosa, K. pneumoniae, E. faecium, S. aureus, C. acnes. Supplementary Table S1: antibiotic control MICs (not extracted as peptide records). Assay: broth microdilution, MHB. 6 pathogens × 11 peptides = 66 MIC records.

scientific_papers.notes: Primary paper source. Open access CC BY 4.0. Covers 6 rationally designed AMPs (R1, R10–R14) and 5 reference AMPs (RP556, LZ1, AA139, PA13, Oligo10) against ESKAPE pathogens + C. acnes. All 6 pathogens are clinically relevant. Sequences and MICs in same paper — no cross-referencing needed. Broth microdilution method described in Materials section.

scientific_papers.name: Wang et al. 2024 — Novel Symmetrical AMPs against Resistant Clinical Isolates (PMC11537005)
scientific_papers.access_method: open-access PDF via PMC or Frontiers (CC BY 4.0)

extraction_strategy.notes: MIC table: 5 peptides (W1–W5) vs. S. aureus ATCC 29213, MRSA 544, MRSA 1022, MRSA 10243, E. coli ATCC 25922, P. aeruginosa ATCC 9027, S. epidermidis ATCC 12228. Units in µM — requires conversion to µg/mL using molecular weight. Also includes vancomycin and melittin controls. Sequences in Table or supplementary.

scientific_papers.notes: Provides MRSA clinical isolate data, which is underrepresented in databases. MIC reported in µM — requires unit conversion (record molecular_weight_da for each peptide). WWW motif peptides. Covers both Gram-positive and Gram-negative standard strains plus clinical MRSA isolates.

scientific_papers.name: Kim et al. 2023 — T. ni Cecropin A vs Colistin-Resistant Escherichia coli

extraction_strategy.notes: Table 1: MIC in µg/mL for T. ni cecropin A, H. cecropia cecropin A, polymyxin B, colistin, melittin against 8 strains: E. coli ATCC 25922, A. baumannii ATCC 19606, P. aeruginosa ATCC 27853, K. pneumoniae ATCC 13883 + 4 colistin-resistant clinical isolates. Record peptide MIC rows only (polymyxin/colistin are antibiotics, not peptides — exclude from dataset). Sequences in Supplementary.

scientific_papers.notes: Unique for colistin-resistant pathogen coverage — relevant for AMR context. T. ni cecropin A (insect AMP, Trichoplusia ni). H. cecropia cecropin A is the classical reference. Provides a natural AMP benchmark. MIC units µg/mL. Assay: broth microdilution, MHB. Only extract rows for T. ni cecropin and cecropin A (exclude antibiotic controls).

supplementary_materials.name: Luo et al. 2023 — Supplementary Table S1 (antibiotic controls)
supplementary_materials.access_method: supplementary PDF download from MDPI article page

extraction_strategy.notes: Antibiotic (non-peptide) MIC controls only. Do NOT extract as peptide records — out of dataset scope. Useful for cross-checking assay conditions and confirming pathogen susceptibility profiles.

supplementary_materials.notes: Included for completeness and cross-validation. Antibiotic MIC values against the same pathogen panel confirm assay validity. Not extracted as primary records.

aggregators.name: UniProt — Universal Protein Resource

extraction_strategy.notes: Query by peptide name or sequence to retrieve canonical source organism binomial names and UniProt accessions. Use /uniprotkb endpoint. Enrich organism_source field — does not provide MIC values.

aggregators.notes: Metadata enrichment only. Does not provide MIC values. Used to standardize organism_source field to canonical binomial species names (e.g., 'Homo sapiens' instead of 'human').

aggregators.name: NCBI Taxonomy — National Center for Biotechnology Information
aggregators.access_method: Entrez API (Biopython Entrez module); no authentication required beyond NCBI email registration

extraction_strategy.notes: Fetch canonical species name and taxon ID for each pathogen_name. Used to standardize pathogen names across sources. Gram-stain classification looked up from taxon lineage (Firmicutes → Gram+; Proteobacteria → Gram−).

aggregators.notes: Metadata enrichment for pathogen fields. Provides canonical pathogen_name and supports gram_stain classification. Does not provide MIC values.

github_repositories.name: CPU-DRAMP/DRAMP-3.0 — DRAMP 3.0 Source Data on GitHub
github_repositories.access_method: git clone or ZIP download; Excel file in repository root

extraction_strategy.notes: Excel workbook contains sequences from 4 databases (DRAMP, APD, DBAASP, CAMP). Cross-reference commit hash for reproducibility. Filter to antibacterial entries with experimental MIC values. Note: this is a snapshot of DRAMP 3.0 data — superseded by DRAMP 4.0 on the download page. Use as provenance cross-check only.

github_repositories.notes: Secondary access path for DRAMP data. Useful when the DRAMP downloads page is unavailable. The Excel file covers sequences aggregated from 4 major AMP databases. Verify license: CC BY 4.0 from DRAMP. Record the commit SHA for reproducibility.

ml_datasets.name: QMAP Benchmark 2025 — Quantitative MIC/HC50 Regression Benchmark (DBAASP-derived)
ml_datasets.access_method: Hugging Face datasets library: `datasets.load_dataset('anthol42/qmap_benchmark_2025')` or pip install qmap
ml_datasets.license: research use (derived from DBAASP; check original DBAASP license for redistribution)

extraction_strategy.notes: QMAP provides pre-split MIC regression datasets for E. coli and S. aureus derived from DBAASP v3. Do NOT use as primary MIC source — use for cross-validation against db_dbaasp records only. Filter to antibacterial (non-antifungal) peptides. Verify each record against the original DBAASP entry by peptide ID before including.

ml_datasets.notes: Cross-validation source only. QMAP provides homology-aware benchmark splits for MIC prediction, derived from DBAASP v3. Published in biorXiv 2026 (doi: 10.64898/2026.02.03.703041). Contains MIC values for E. coli (5 datasets) and S. aureus (2 datasets). Each original DBAASP ID is retained, allowing back-referencing. Do not treat as an independent source — records duplicate db_dbaasp; use to detect discrepancies.

reference_databases.name: APD6 — Antimicrobial Peptide Database (version 6)
reference_databases.access_method: web search and FASTA download (https://aps.unmc.edu/downloads); no bulk MIC export currently available
reference_databases.license: free for research and education (cite APD3/APD6)

extraction_strategy.notes: APD6 MIC data is being unified and validated (per APD FAQ as of Jan 2026) — not yet available as a bulk MIC export. Use APD6 only for sequence cross-validation (checking peptide_sequence against canonical APD ID) and peptide name standardization. Do not extract as primary MIC records.

reference_databases.notes: Reference database only — NOT a primary MIC source for this project. APD6 (Jan 2026) contains 6,309 peptides (3,379 natural + 2,290 synthetic + 373 predicted). MIC bulk export is pending unification per the APD FAQ. Useful for verifying peptide sequences and names retrieved from DBAASP or DRAMP against the canonical APD entry.
